title: Hoops
tagline: A different kind of Social Media — connect through anonymous letter-based chatting.

screenshot.alt: The home surface, stripped of everything optional.

aboutHeadline: A social product designed around what it refuses to do.
blurb: Hoops is an anonymous social network built around long-form letters instead of photos, profiles, or performative posts. No profile pictures, no follower counts, no public timelines — just 600-character minimum letters routed by shared interests and Myers-Briggs compatibility. The constraints are the product: removing the usual social levers forces the conversation to carry its own weight.
pullQuotes: Remove the profile picture and the follower count, and what's left is the part that was supposed to matter all along.
Anonymity isn't the feature. Slowness is.
Designed around what it refuses to do.
highlightsHeadline: The four pieces that make Hoops, Hoops.

highlights.title: Anonymous letter sending
highlights.lede: The atomic unit of Hoops is a letter, not a post.
highlights.body: A 600-character floor forces users to slow down and actually say something. A live counter tracks length without nagging, and the composer strips away every social signal that would normally crowd a message — no avatars, no read receipts, no typing indicators.

shots.caption: A live counter quietly tracks length.
Letters render on a canvas, not a feed.
Threads collapse into a single conversation per pen-pal.

highlights.title: Interest & personality routing
highlights.lede: Pen-pals are matched, not searched for.
highlights.body: A lightweight Myers-Briggs intake plus tagged interests power a routing layer that suggests pen-pals without ever exposing a profile. The match surface stays small on purpose — three suggestions at a time, refreshed weekly.

shots.caption: Three pen-pals at a time. Weekly cadence.

highlights.title: Live-validated signup
highlights.body: Real-time username validation runs on every keystroke against a debounced staging endpoint, with a final production check on submit. The flow is two screens long — name, then interests — and avatar selection is randomized to keep self-presentation out of the loop.

shots.caption: Optional class schedule for real-world overlap.

highlights.title: Designing for absence
highlights.lede: Every screen earns its right to exist.
highlights.body: The home surface shows what's new — and nothing else. No badges, no streaks, no algorithmic feed. Settings live behind a single drawer. The product's identity is built on what was deliberately left out, and the UI reflects that with whitespace, not ornament.

shots.caption: The home surface, stripped of everything optional.
The brand mark — present, then absent.

gallery.alt: Login and signup with live validation
gallery.label: Login · auth

gallery.alt: Friend suggestions based on interests and personality
gallery.label: Suggestions · matching

challenges.title: Validating usernames in real time without burning the DB
challenges.body: Every keystroke on the signup field wanted to hit Mongo. A debounce + a staging collection for in-flight checks let the UI feel instant while production stayed quiet — and only the final submit touched the real users table. The lesson was that perceived responsiveness and backend load are two different problems, and they need two different solutions.

challenges.title: Matching on personality without performing surveillance
challenges.body: Myers-Briggs is a blunt tool, but it's a tool people opt into. Pairing it with tagged interests gave the matching layer enough signal to make sensible suggestions without ever asking for a bio, a photo, or a friend graph. The product worked better with less data, not more.

challenges.title: Designing for the absence of identity
challenges.body: Most social UI patterns assume an avatar and a name to anchor every interaction. Stripping those out broke a lot of defaults — empty states, threading, even the visual rhythm of a list. The fix was to lean further into restraint, not to backfill ornament. The product's identity is built on what isn't there.
